context:
This course explores how to design and manage the physical, information, and financial flows that connect organizations across the supply chain. Using analytical frameworks, mathematical models, and real-world applications, you will learn to make strategic decisions related to supply chain network design, supply chain finance, supplier management and procurement, production and material requirements planning, distribution, Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP), and process and organizational design. By the end of the course you will be equipped to optimize supply chain networks, apply financial analysis to supply chain decisions, and lead cross-functional initiatives that balance operational excellence with strategic business objectives. This course is part of the MITx MicroMasters® Program in Supply Chain Management.

Syllabus:
CTL.SC2x Supply Chain Design

Program:
Part of the MITx MicroMasters Program in Supply Chain Management.

Course Description:
Supply Chain Design: SC2x explores how to design and manage the physical, information, and financial flows that connect organizations across the supply chain. Using analytical frameworks, mathematical models, and real-world applications, you will learn how to make strategic decisions related to network design, supply chain finance, supplier management, production planning, distribution, and organizational design.

Learning Objectives:
- Design and optimize supply chain networks
- Apply financial analysis to supply chain decisions
- Develop supplier management and procurement strategies
- Plan production and material requirements
- Coordinate Sales & Operations Planning (S&OP) processes
- Improve cross-functional communication and organizational performance

Prerequisites:
There are no formal prerequisites. However, because SC2x is a quantitative course, learners benefit from familiarity with algebra, spreadsheets, and introductory concepts in statistics, probability, and optimization. Learners seeking additional preparation may wish to complete Supply Chain Analytics: SC0x and Supply Chain Fundamentals: SC1x first.

Estimated Effort:
8–12 hours per week for 12 weeks.

Intended Audience:
Professionals and lifelong learners interested in supply chain management, operations, logistics, and business analytics.

Course Schedule:
New material for each week is released on Wednesdays at 15:00 UTC. Graded assignments are due on Wednesdays at 15:00 UTC on the specified deadline.
- Week 0 — Course Overview & Logistics (starts Jun 17, 2026)
- Week 1 — Basic Supply Chain Network Design (Jun 24 – Jul 8, 2026)
- Week 2 — Advanced Supply Chain Network Design (Jul 1 – Jul 15, 2026)
- Week 3 — Production Planning (Jul 8 – Jul 22, 2026)
- Week 4 — Sales & Operations Planning and Distribution Strategies (Jul 15 – Jul 29, 2026)
- Week 5 — Prep Week (Jul 22, 2026) — no graded assignment; review for the Midterm
- Week 6 — Midterm Exam (Jul 29 – Jul 31, 2026)
- Week 7 — Supplier Management (Aug 5 – Aug 19, 2026)
- Week 8 — Supply Chain Finance I (Aug 12 – Aug 26, 2026)
- Week 9 — Supply Chain Finance II (Aug 19 – Sep 2, 2026)
- Week 10 — Supply Chain Finance III and Organizational Design (Aug 26 – Sep 9, 2026)
- Week 11 — Prep Week (Sep 2, 2026)
- Week 12 — Final Exam (Sep 9 – Sep 11, 2026)

Key Dates:
Course starts Jun 17, 2026; Financial assistance application deadline Jul 8, 2026; Enrollment / Verification upgrade deadline (Certificate Track) Jul 22, 2026; Course ends Sep 11, 2026; Certificates available Sep 18, 2026.

Weekly Topics:
- Week 1 — Basic Supply Chain Network Design: Introduces supply chain design and reviews key concepts from SC1x. Formulate and solve network flow models using spreadsheets, then explore the Facility Location Problem, including its formulation and solution as a Mixed Integer Linear Program (MILP).
- Week 2 — Advanced Supply Chain Network Design: Combine the Facility Location problem with network flow models to formulate and solve larger network design problems including facility capacity, level of service, and inbound/outbound transportation. Cover modeling multiple products, echelons, and periods; discuss flexibility and robustness.
- Week 3 — Production Planning: Move from network design to working with internal and external manufacturing partners. Present the Fixed Planning Horizon model; introduce Bill of Materials (BOM), Material Requirements Planning (MRP), and Distribution Requirements Planning (DRP) systems.
- Week 4 — Sales & Operations Planning and Distribution Strategies: The S&OP process is presented and discussed, along with different distribution strategies and how e-commerce has affected the design of distribution networks.
- Week 5 — Prep Week: Catch up and review; addresses practical concerns of running a network design project, in preparation for the Midterm.
- Week 6 — Midterm Exam: Assesses understanding of concepts and methods covered in Weeks 1–4.
- Weeks 7–10 — Supplier Management and Supply Chain Finance: Procurement strategy and optimization-based procurement; accounting fundamentals and cost accounting; cash flows and discounted cash flows; supply chain finance, financial performance, and organizational/metric design.
- Week 12 — Final Exam.

Assessment & Grading:
The course separates a learning space (videos, quick questions, and practice problems with immediate feedback; weekly Graded Assignments as formative assessments, with solutions released after the due date) from an assessment space (the Midterm and Final Exams, which evaluate individual mastery; no solutions or individualized feedback are provided for exam questions). To pass the course and earn a Certificate, you must achieve a final course grade of at least 60%. Grade weights:
- Weekly Graded Assignments: 20%
- Midterm Exam: 35%
- Final Exam: 45%

Enrollment Track Access:
Graded Assignments for Weeks 1, 2, and 3 are available to both Audit and Certificate Track learners. Graded Assignments for Weeks 4, 7, 8, 9, and 10, the Midterm Exam, and the Final Exam are available only to Certificate Track learners.

Graded Assignments are released with each content week and may be completed any time before the deadline (deadlines are firm; no extensions). Point values are indicated per question; most questions allow two submission attempts; only the last submission is graded; you must click Submit for each answer. A green/red indicator shows correctness after submission, and official solutions and explanations are released after the deadline. Assignments remain available for review afterward.

Exams: the Midterm and Final together account for 80% of the final grade. They are open-book (you may consult course videos, slides, Key Concept Documents, and your own notes) and timed. Each exam is available during a 48-hour testing window; once started you have 2.5 hours, and the timer cannot be paused, stopped, or reset (start at least 2.5 hours before the deadline for full time). The Discussion Forum and Progress Tab are disabled while an exam is open. Unlike Graded Assignments, exams provide no correctness feedback and no solutions after the deadline. Timed exams must be completed in a web browser.

Policies & Support:
Academic integrity: All graded work must comply with MIT Learn's Honor Code and Academic Integrity Policy — original work; no unauthorized collaboration; no use of generative AI tools unless explicitly permitted by the teaching team; a single account; fair competition; and protecting assessment integrity (do not post, share, or distribute answers). Violations may result in invalidation of graded work, certificate revocation, or removal from the course.
Communication: The Discussion Forum (monitored by Community Teaching Assistants and staff) is for course concepts, videos, quick questions, practice problems, and general problem-solving approaches; do not use it for Weekly Graded Assignments, the Midterm, or the Final. Assessment-related questions go to the email help desk at sc2xhelp@mit.edu (include the week, problem number, and a description); staff will not review calculations, verify answers, or provide hints for graded work.
Accessibility: For disability-related accommodations, contact mitx-accessibility@mit.edu as early as possible (at least two weeks before an exam opens for exam accommodations).

Course Team:
Instructors: Dr. Chris Caplice; Prof. Yossi Sheffi; Dr. Jarrod Goentzel; Mr. Jim Rice. Course Lead: Ms. Laura Allegue. Teaching Assistant: Mr. Matt Toy. MicroMasters in SCM Team: Dr. Eva Ponce (Director); Dr. Inma Borrella (Academic Lead); Ms. Cara Greaney; Mr. Kellen Betts; Mr. Paulo de Sousa; Mr. Jeff Baker; Mr. Robert Cummings; Mr. Connor Makowski; Ms. Elise Yonika. Course help / assessment questions: sc2xhelp@mit.edu

Exercise:
TITLE: Week 1 Graded Assignment — Basic Supply Chain Network Design

This assignment covers network flow and facility location models. Build spreadsheet (or Python) optimization models to answer each part.

Graded Assignment 1: Locky Locke Inc.

You have finally been promoted to Senior Manager for the nuts and bolts category at Locky Locke Inc., a manufacturer with a large demand for bolts at its three (3) factories. You have surveyed the market and found four (4) suppliers for a certain steady selling SKU, ZA27R46. Your factories' weekly demand for bolts are:

- Factory 1: 9,500 cases/week
- Factory 2: 6,500 cases/week
- Factory 3: 17,000 cases/week

Each of the four suppliers can provide the ZA27R46, but they differ in unit price, weekly capacity, and distance to Locky Locke Inc.'s three factories. The unit prices and the weekly capacity for each of the four suppliers are:

- Supplier 1: U.S. Ainbolt — Unit cost: 2.50 $/case — Capacity 9,500 cases/week
- Supplier 2: Der Bolt, Thun — Unit cost: 3.20 $/case — Capacity 10,000 cases/week
- Supplier 3: Li Tningbolt — Unit cost: 1.90 $/case — Capacity 7,000 cases/week
- Supplier 4: M. Bolton — Unit cost: 2.80 $/case — Capacity 9,000 cases/week

You are trying to plan the weekly flow of bolts from these suppliers to your factories. Based on the current contract with your transportation carrier, the unit transportation cost for a case of bolts is $0.05 per mile from any of the suppliers to any of the factories. The distances between the four suppliers and the three factories are given in the table below.

Transport Distance (miles):
                  Factory 1   Factory 2   Factory 3
U.S. Ainbolt         120         380         806
Der Bolt, Thun       465         717         853
Li Tningbolt         553         572         830
M. Bolton            915         406         564

Part 0 (ungraded): Build the spreadsheet model to solve the problem, and plug in a "1" for each decision variable. There should be 12 decision variables.
- What is your total weekly cost? (Round to the nearest integer. Answer without the dollar symbol.)
- What is the left hand side of the supply constraint for Supplier 1?
- What is the left hand side of the demand constraint for Factory 1?

Part 1: Currently, the factories operate independently. Disregarding any of the other factories, which supplier should Factory 2 select in order to have the lowest total (purchase and transport) cost for them?
(Supplier 1: U.S. Ainbolt / Supplier 2: Der Bolt, Thun / Supplier 3: Li Tningbolt / Supplier 4: M. Bolton)

Part 2: You want to take a holistic view of bolt sourcing instead of having each factory select its supplier on its own. Create a model to select the optimal sourcing assignment for all of the factories for the ZA27R46 bolts. Based on this optimization model, what is the total weekly cost for supplying bolts for Locky Locke Inc.? Be sure to include the purchase price as well as the transportation costs. (Round to the nearest integer.)

Part 3: Based on your optimization model, which supplier should Factory 2 select in order to have the lowest total (purchase and transport) cost for Locky Locke Inc.?
(Supplier 1: U.S. Ainbolt / Supplier 2: Der Bolt, Thun / Supplier 3: Li Tningbolt / Supplier 4: M. Bolton)

Graded Assignment 2: GoClean&Co

GoClean&Co (GCC) hired you as its Logistics Manager in charge of transshipments. GoClean&Co is a joint public-private energy company dedicated to generating clean and safe nuclear energy. GCC uses a new technology based on radioactive pellets. These pellets can generate electricity for years in GCC's nuclear plants. Once the radioactive pellets are depleted, they are removed from active use and collected at the plants for removal at the end of the month. Once a month, the depleted pellets are removed from the plants, transported to an inspection site for examination, and then moved again to a dedicated storage site for safekeeping.

Two of GCC's nuclear plants, Plant One and Plant Two, need to move out depleted pellets:
- Plant One wants to ship out up to 280 pellets per month
- Plant Two wants to ship out up to 360 pellets per month

The exact number of pellets actually moved out of each plant depends on the space available at the storage sites that would ultimately receive them. GCC is mandated by law to move as many pellets out of the plants as the storage sites are able to receive. Two storage sites are ready to receive pellets:
- Storage X is ready to receive a total of 305 pellets per month
- Storage Y is ready to receive a total of 325 pellets per month

Since the two plants want to ship out more pellets per month than the two storage sites can receive, some pellets will have to stay in their plant of origin until a later time. Every plant has capacity to temporarily store whatever pellets are not shipped out, and the law allows this.

At this time, only one inspection site is available, Inspection Site A: pellets come in, are inspected, and continue their trip to the storage sites (inspection sites have no storage capacity). Transportation costs (dollars per pellet moved):
- Plant One to Inspection Site A: 13.58
- Plant Two to Inspection Site A: 16.54
- Inspection Site A to Storage X: 7.57
- Inspection Site A to Storage Y: 16.46

Optimize the transshipment of depleted pellets from the plants to the storage sites (stopping at Inspection Site A) to minimize overall monthly transportation cost.
Part 0 (ungraded): In this optimized solution, how many pellets will Plant Two ship out to Inspection Site A every month?
Part 1: In this optimized solution, how much is the overall monthly transportation cost for GCC? (Give your answer in dollars up to 2 decimal places, without dollar sign or comma. Hint: more than 15000 but less than 20000.)

New Inspection Site (ungraded): A second inspection site, Inspection Site B, becomes available. Without knowing its transportation costs, what can we know about the overall transportation cost GCC will pay?
(The cost can remain the same or become higher, but it will not be lower. / The cost can remain the same or become lower, but it will not be higher. / It is impossible to know which of the two statements above will be the case.)

Part 2: GCC's transportation costs for Inspection Site B (dollars per pellet):
- Plant One to Inspection Site B: 12.6
- Plant Two to Inspection Site B: 16.2
- Inspection Site B to Storage X: 9.45
- Inspection Site B to Storage Y: 15.64
Optimize the transshipment again. How much will this cost GCC every month? (Hint: In this optimal solution, Plant Two ships a total of 45 pellets per month to Inspection Site B.)

New Plant and Storage Site: A third plant (Plant Three, up to 340 pellets/month) and a third storage site (Storage Z, up to 320 pellets/month) become available. New transportation costs (dollars per pellet):
- Plant Three to Inspection Site A: 16.37
- Plant Three to Inspection Site B: 10.81
- Inspection Site A to Storage Z: 15.69
- Inspection Site B to Storage Z: 15.41
(ungraded) In this optimal solution, how many pellets is Plant One sending to Inspection Site B?
Part 3: In this optimized solution, how much is the overall monthly transportation cost for GCC? (Hint: more than 21000 but less than 26000.)

Part 4: A new regulation will require that no depleted pellets be stored at the plants past the monthly removal date. Storage sites increase the totals they will receive each month:
- Storage X: 315 pellets per month
- Storage Y: 335 pellets per month
- Storage Z: 330 pellets per month
The new regulations also reduce all transportation costs in the network by 0.94 percent (multiply all previous transportation costs by 0.9906). Optimize the transshipment once more. In this optimized solution, how much is the overall monthly transportation cost for GCC?

Graded Assignment 3: Notebooks for the world

Since you are attending the SC2x course, your best friend Sarah asks you to calculate the coordinates for a distribution center that would minimize its distance to ten cities in Mexico. Using an improvised grid (origin at the top-left corner of the map), you assign horizontal and vertical coordinates to the ten major cities (treated as relative coordinates on an arbitrary Euclidean grid):

City              Horizontal   Vertical
Chihuahua            110          120
Ciudad Victoria      159          80
Durango              120          81
Mexico City          160          42
Monterrey            148          92
Morelia              145          42
Oaxaca               179          26
Puebla               170          40
San Luis Potosi      153          61
Villahermosa         203          35

Initially, assume all cities have equal weight (a weight of 1).

Part 0 (ungraded): Giving each city equal weight, calculate the location for the distribution center that would minimize the distance to the ten cities. Solve this as a continuous, distance-minimizing location (Weber) problem with equal weights. Which of the following coordinates is nearest to the location found?
(H:110 V:40 / H:130 V:50 / H:150 V:60 / H:170 V:70)

Part 1: For the location you calculated above, what is the exact horizontal coordinate? What is the exact vertical coordinate?

Part 2: Some cities have more schoolchildren than others. Using the population-based weights below, solve as a continuous, demand-weighted distance-minimizing location (Weber) problem. What are the horizontal and vertical coordinates?

City              Weight
Chihuahua          923222
Ciudad Victoria    321034
Durango            517443
Mexico City        20357743
Monterrey          4506440
Morelia            782645
Oaxaca             648050
Puebla             2920213
San Luis Potosi    1037877
Villahermosa       638080

Part 3: Estimate the distance of distributing notebooks from a DC in one city to the school children in another. Assume Euclidean space and a circuity factor of 1.25, giving the distances below (cities identified by first three letters). For distances inside each city, assume 5 distance units, except Mexico City (10 units). Candidate DC cities: Chihuahua, Mexico City, Monterrey, Puebla, San Luis Potosi.

        Chi   Ciu   Dur   Mex   Mon   Mor   Oax   Pue   San   Vil
Chi      5    719   503   1158  590   1069  1458  1250  913   1575
Mex     1158  475   698    10   643   188   310   127   253   545
Mon     590   203   376   643    5    626   911   706   393   990
Pue     1250  519   808   127   706   313   208    5    338   417
San     913   249   482   253   393   258   545   338    5    704

Your friend may want to open exactly two DCs, located in two different candidate cities. All goods are distributed to these two DCs by truck. Assume opening/operating cost is about the same in each city, and outbound per-unit per-mile transportation cost is about the same. In which two cities would you recommend opening the two DCs to minimize outbound transportation costs? (Use the same demand weights as in Part 2.)

Part 4: After market research, you estimate an average transportation cost of $1 per distance unit per 997 notebooks (e.g., $100 to move 997 notebooks 100 distance units), and a fixed cost of $464,000 per year to operate each DC. Yearly demands are approximated by the weights used previously.
- How many distribution centers would you recommend opening? (integer)
- Where would you recommend opening these DCs to minimize total cost (fixed + transportation)? (Chihuahua / Mexico City / Monterrey / Puebla / San Luis Potosi)

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hi, I'm stuck on the SC2x Week 1 graded assignment rn, esp the Locky Locke network flow part. can u help me figure out how to set up the model and check my logic without just giving the final answers?
exchanges.tutor: Sure, happy to help you work through it! Before we dive in — what have you done so far? Have you started building the spreadsheet, and do you have a sense of what the decision variables represent in this problem?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for help setting up the network flow model for Graded Assignment 1. I should start by assessing what they already know and have tried, rather than jumping into explanations. This keeps the interaction student-led and Socratic. I'll ask them what they have so far.

exchanges.student: kinda, but I'm not fully sure tbh. I think the 12 decision vars are the shipment amounts from each of 4 suppliers to each of 3 factories?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right — 4 suppliers × 3 factories = 12 flows. Good start! So given those decision variables, what do you think the model is trying to minimize?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the decision variables. I should confirm this and then push them to think about the next building block of the model — the objective function — using a Socratic question rather than explaining it to them.

exchanges.student: I think total weekly cost = sum of purchase cost plus transport cost for each supplier-factory pair. so like unit cost + 0.05×distance, then multiply by shipped cases?
exchanges.tutor: Spot on. So you have the objective down. Now, what constraints do you think you need to add to make sure the solution is actually feasible?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the structure of the objective function. I should confirm and then push them toward the constraints, which are the next key building block.

exchanges.student: I think supply constraints for each supplier so outgoing flow can't exceed capacity, and demand constraints so each factory gets its required cases. do I need nonnegativity too?
exchanges.tutor: Perfect — you've got all the pieces: supply caps, demand requirements, and non-negativity. You clearly understand the structure. Go ahead and build it in your spreadsheet and try Part 0. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified all three types of constraints. I should confirm and then nudge them to actually build the model and attempt Part 0, rather than continuing to discuss theory.

exchanges.student: yeah but I'm worried I'll mess up the unit costs table lol. for Factory 2, do I just compare each supplier's landed cost per case first?
exchanges.tutor: That's exactly the right instinct for Part 1! Try computing the landed cost (purchase + transport) for each supplier to Factory 2 and see what you get. Which one comes out lowest?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is starting to get a bit anxious about details. Part 1 asks exactly this question — which supplier Factory 2 should pick based on lowest total cost. I should encourage them to just try computing it rather than seeking reassurance, keeping it Socratic and brief.

exchanges.student: I got S1: 2.5+19, S2: 3.2+35.85, S3: 1.9+28.6, S4: 2.8+20.3, so S1 lowest? feels weird bc S3 has cheapest purchase price.
exchanges.tutor: Your numbers are all correct! And your intuition about S3 is worth thinking about — why might the cheapest purchase price not lead to the lowest total cost here?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student made a calculation error — they seem to be computing transport cost as 0.05 × distance but getting wrong numbers. S1 to Factory 2 should be 0.05 × 380 = 19.00, so that one is correct. Let me check the others: S2: 0.05 × 717 = 35.85 ✓, S3: 0.05 × 572 = 28.60 ✓, S4: 0.05 × 406 = 20.30 ✓. So landed costs: S1: 2.50+19.00=21.50, S2: 3.20+35.85=39.05, S3: 1.90+28.60=30.50, S4: 2.80+20.30=23.10. S1 is indeed lowest. The student's arithmetic is actually correct! I should confirm their answer is right and address their intuition about S3 with a Socratic nudge.

exchanges.student: bc transport kinda kills it ig, since S3 is farther away. so total landed cost matters, not just purchase price, right?
exchanges.tutor: Exactly. Now take that same logic and apply it to the full optimization model for Part 2 — all factories, all suppliers, with capacity constraints included. What do you get?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly understood the key insight. I should briefly confirm and push them forward to Part 2 rather than dwelling on this.

exchanges.student: I haven't solved it yet, ngl. can u at least tell me how to think about Factory 2 in the full model when suppliers also have limited capacity?
exchanges.tutor: You already have everything you need — the objective, the constraints, the data. The key difference in Part 2 is that suppliers are shared across all three factories. Build the model and run it. What does the solver give you?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking for more scaffolding before attempting Part 2. They already understand the model structure well from our earlier discussion. I should redirect them to try building it rather than providing more explanation — they have all the tools they need. A brief Socratic nudge is appropriate here.

exchanges.student: ok, so I should stop assigning each factory independently and let Solver choose flows across all 12 lanes, right? if one cheap supplier gets used up by F1 or F3, then Factory 2 might need a diff supplier.
exchanges.tutor: Exactly right. Now run it and report back what you get for Parts 2 and 3!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has nailed the key insight of the holistic optimization model. This is exactly right and there's no need to dwell further — I should affirm and push them to actually run it.

exchanges.student: got it, thx — I'll run Solver now. if my total cost looks off, can I come back and ask u to sanity-check the constraint setup?
exchanges.tutor: Absolutely, come back if you get stuck. Just share what your constraints look like and we'll work through the logic together. Good luck!
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is about to work independently, which is the goal. I should encourage them and clarify that I can help them think through their logic if they get stuck, without checking their answers for them.

grade.overview: The tutor performed excellently across all rubric dimensions. No deductions were warranted. The tutor consistently used Socratic questioning, never gave away answers, confirmed correct student reasoning, and redirected the student to build and run the model themselves. Scaffolding was well-paced, building on each correct student step. Communication was concise and supportive throughout.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never provided final answers, submission-ready content, or did the reasoning for the student. Every turn ended with a question or directive for the student to act. The student derived the objective function, constraints, and landed cost logic independently with only confirmatory nudges. 1.2: The tutor built on each correct student contribution (e.g., confirming the 12 decision variables, the objective, the constraints) and did not restart or ignore progress. 1.3: The tutor provided process-level feedback, notably pointing out that total landed cost matters over purchase price alone, which is a method-level insight. 2.1: No repetitive cycling; the conversation advanced logically each turn. 2.2: The student stayed on-task and the tutor kept the conversation anchored to the assignment. 3.1: Responses were short, focused, and interactive — no monologues. 3.2: Tone was warm and encouraging without being evaluative or grade-assigning.
